name: Ingredient Quality
description: Ingredients carry grade metadata that can affect recipe eligibility, output stats, yield, failure, or rarity.
design_notes: Ingredient quality adds depth only when the grade affects decisions players can understand. Recipes need compatibility tags, substitution rules, quality weight curves, deterministic roll seeds, and accurate UI previews so players know whether rare ingredients improve output or are being wasted.
design_purpose: Give gathered materials different value without requiring entirely separate recipe lists.
Let players choose between saving rare ingredients, improving output, or substituting lower-grade materials.
Create a bridge between gathering, economy, crafting, and progression systems.
player_fantasy: The player feels that better ingredients matter and can deliberately craft superior results.

parameters.name: ingredient_quality_tier
parameters.description: Grade label stored on ingredient item instances and checked by recipe compatibility rules.

parameters.name: quality_weight_curve_id
parameters.description: Curve that converts ingredient grade mix into output quality or stat modifiers.

parameters.name: material_substitution_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides which alternate ingredient ids may satisfy a recipe slot.

parameters.name: output_quality_seed
parameters.description: Deterministic seed used when output grade includes a bounded random roll.

parameters.name: recipe_compatibility_tag
parameters.description: Tag matched between ingredient instances and recipe slots before preview or confirmation.

edge_cases: A stack contains mixed item_instance_id quality tiers but the UI treats it as one uniform ingredient.
Preview uses the highest possible output while server validation uses the actual selected ingredients.
A recipe accepts substitutions but the output name, stats, or icon still assume the default material.
Content migration changes tier names and old saved ingredients no longer match compatibility tags.
common_bugs: Ingredient quality is stored only on item definitions, so every instance of the material has the same grade.
Random output rolls are not seeded by transaction_id and produce different results on client and server.
The crafting UI auto-selects rare ingredients for low-value recipes without warning the player.
Stack splitting drops quality metadata and converts superior materials into default-grade items.
balancing_notes: Quality tiers are most useful when they change meaningful stats or yields, not only item color.
Low-tier materials should remain useful through bulk recipes, repairs, or substitutions so gathering stays rewarding.
If output improvement is random, publish the expected result or range so players can make informed choices.
accessibility_notes: Represent quality with text labels and icons, not color alone.
Warn clearly before consuming high-grade ingredients in recipes where grade has little or no effect.

implementation_notes.unity: Store ingredient_quality_tier on item_instance_id records, not only ScriptableObject item definitions. The recipe preview and confirm transaction should call the same quality_weight_curve_id code path and include selected instance ids in any server request.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Resources for tier rules and item metadata, but keep per-instance quality in inventory save data. Control UI should preview substitution and output grade from the exact selected stack entries, then lock those entries during confirmation.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Gameplay Tags or data assets for compatibility tags, with per-instance inventory metadata for grade. Server crafting should resolve substitution, deterministic output_quality_seed, and output stat generation before replicating the new item instance.
implementation_notes.web: Represent ingredient stacks as arrays or grouped records that preserve grade counts. Avoid client-only grade rolls; use transaction_id plus output_quality_seed so previews, saves, and multiplayer confirmations stay reproducible.

example_games.title: Monster Hunter: World
example_games.note: Uses gathered monster parts as valued ingredients for equipment crafting.

example_games.title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
example_games.note: Uses ingredient properties and crafting inputs as part of alchemy and equipment loops.
